Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time, but with limited resources at home and my extracurricular commitments, I ended up studying whenever I could grab a moment. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often be doing things physically—writing notes, pacing while reading—rather than sitting at a desk for hours. Some weeks I'd put in solid time, other weeks barely anything until something was due.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was coming up. I'd have these bursts of activity right before deadlines, then go quiet for a while. Regular times? Not really my thing. Between my activities outside of class and just life stuff, I couldn't stick to a fixed schedule even when I tried.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline creeping up or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, especially if I hadn't been to class in a while. The gamified tasks actually got me curious sometimes—I'd want to see my progress or unlock something, which was surprisingly motivating.
Q4: Yeah, there was a period mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic for me. The gamified elements helped with that—I'd want to check my points or see where I stood. It felt less like a chore and more like checking a game. That routine kind of faded when things got stressful, but for a while it was genuinely part of my day.
Q5: The video content was huge for me—I watched over a hundred minutes of it because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace. The gamified tasks were also great because they made learning feel interactive rather than passive. As a kinesthetic learner, anything that let me actively do something rather than just read was way more effective.
Q6: I'd often skip the lengthy reading materials and some of the discussion forums. The readings felt overwhelming when I was already pressed for time, and I didn't always have the energy to engage deeply in discussions. I'd skim them or download them intending to read later, but 'later' didn't always come. It wasn't that they weren't useful—I just had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or when it connected to something practical. The gamified stuff worked because there was instant feedback—points, badges, progress bars. Also, anything that I knew would directly help with assignments or exams felt worthwhile. If something seemed abstract or disconnected, I'd push it aside.
Q8: Definitely different. Required activities had this weight to them—I'd do them even if I wasn't feeling it because I had to. Optional ones? I'd only do them if they genuinely interested me or if the gamification made them appealing. Sometimes optional stuff was actually more engaging because there was no pressure, but I'd often skip them when stressed.
Q9: All the time, actually. I'd download videos and PDFs to my phone and study them offline because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd also make handwritten notes and review those away from my computer. A lot of my learning happened while I was commuting or during breaks from my extracurriculars—none of that would show up in any system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be going over my notes or rewatching downloaded lectures, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study with friends in person, quizzing each other or working through problems together. My attendance was low, but I was often catching up through other means that didn't leave a digital trace.
Q11: Oh yeah, constantly. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then that was my main study material for days. The LMS would show one view, but I might have gone through that content five or six times offline. It's just more practical for me given my situation with resources and internet access.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when stress hit medium levels and I felt overwhelmed by everything. My resilience isn't the strongest, so when multiple things piled up—assignments, personal stuff, extracurriculars—I'd kind of shut down for a bit. Those were the weeks my attendance dropped even more and I'd barely log in.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was fatigued or had competing responsibilities, the LMS became something I'd only touch when absolutely necessary. Confusion about course material would make me avoid it too—like, if I didn't understand something, I'd procrastinate rather than face it. Workload from other courses meant I'd triage, and sometimes this course got deprioritized.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be logged in, technically 'engaging,' but not absorbing anything. It usually happened when I was tired or stressed—my body was going through the motions but nothing was sticking.
Q15: I postponed things more than I'd like to admit, especially the heavier readings or complex assignments. It wasn't that I didn't know they were important—I just felt overwhelmed and would convince myself I'd do it later. My low resilience means I struggle to push through when things feel too hard, so avoidance became a coping mechanism.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due—suddenly I'm logged in constantly, watching videos, submitting work. Without deadlines, I'd drift. The gamified tasks helped because they created mini-deadlines and goals, which kept me moving even between major due dates.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me. When I got positive feedback or saw my scores improving, I'd feel motivated to keep going. But if feedback was delayed or critical without being constructive, I'd feel deflated and less likely to engage. The immediate feedback from gamified elements was actually more motivating than waiting for assignment grades.
Q18: The structure was a mixed bag. Having 19 online courses meant there was always something to do, which was overwhelming but also meant I could pick what to focus on. The gamified elements gave structure to my chaos—they broke things into manageable chunks. But the overall course load sometimes made me feel like I was drowning.
Q19: The gamification made engagement way easier—it turned studying into something almost fun. But the sheer amount of content and the expectation of regular attendance made it harder. I think if there had been more flexibility built in, or more kinesthetic activities beyond just watching and reading, I would have engaged even more consistently.
Q20: I can totally see how that happens. You might be exploring, trying to understand the platform, or just anxious about missing something. Sometimes high activity is actually confusion—clicking around trying to figure things out. Or maybe someone's dealing with personal stuff and can't focus enough to actually complete work, but they're still trying.
Q21: That's basically me in a nutshell. My attendance was terrible and my login frequency wasn't amazing, but I still pulled a Merit grade. I was learning offline, using downloaded materials, studying in ways that didn't register. The LMS doesn't capture everything—some of us just have different study styles that work outside the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the mental state you're in while studying. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They're a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it, not just complete it. It's when the material clicks and you want to know more. For me, it's when I'm actively doing something with the knowledge—applying it, discussing it, not just passively consuming it. It's a mental and emotional investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline, the conversations I had with classmates outside the platform, or the genuine interest I developed in certain topics. It wouldn't capture my stress levels or how the gamified elements actually shifted my mood and motivation. It definitely wouldn't show that despite my low attendance, I was still committed to doing well.
